content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum:

To : Commissioners Brosnahan, Carpenter,
    Lowenstein, Miller and Waters

Date : June 10, 1975

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Rudy Nothenberg

Subject: Interpretation of Section 84305
         Proposed Regulation or Resolution

75-06-120

We have been asked by the Commission to look into the
questions raised by Controller Kenneth Cory in his Opinion
Request No. 101 and his subsequent appearance before the
Commission. We have also received a memorandum from the
office of Richard Nevins, member of the Board of Equalization, regarding this question, a copy of which is attached
hereto.

The questions deal basically with the nature of those
mailings which might fall within the meter or indicia
requirements of Section 84305. The section provides:

     No person shall make an expenditure for
     the purpose of sending a mass mailing
     unless the postage is paid by postage
     meter or the mail is sent by first class
     or third class bulk rate mail. The bulk
     rate number or meter number shall be
     stated in a campaign statement, and a
     copy of every mass mailing in support
     of or opposition to a state candidate
     or state measure shall be sent to the
     Commission.

This section is an attempt to reduce the opportunity for
anonymous or untraceable mail in campaigns. However, an
overbroad reading of the language would have considerably
wider implications in that it would affect every kind of
mail regardless of whether it was "campaign" mail or not,
and, secondly, in that it forces the use of a particular
mailing method regardless of whether the mail is anonymous
or not.

One possible interpretation of Section 84305 could hold
that any mailing of more than 200 pieces of any kind, whether
paid for by a candidate, a committee, a private person who
is not a committee, paid for by public funds or not, and
regardless of when it was sent, cannot be sent using a postage

stamp is it has "a political purpose" ("Expenditure" defini-
tion in Section 82025). "Political purposes" is not defined
in the Act.

Such an interpretation may be overbroad in that it brings
under the coverage of the Act a whole array of communication,
such as, information about legislative or administrative
action, public statements by officials regarding major news
events, routine press releases unrelated to any campaign for
public office, etc. These serve the function of stimulating
public debate and discourse and are an integral part of
policy formulation. They are likely to be anything but anony-
mous and presumably not the kind of communication from which
Section 84305 intends to protect the public.

A different and somewhat narrower construction of Section
84305, and one which finds some support in the language of
the section, is that only those mailings require the use of
a meter or india which: (1) "support or oppose a state
candidate or state measure;" and (2) are mailings sent by
a person required to file a campaign statement. (Section
84305 uses both terms.)

This interpretation would appear to be consistent with a
common sense interpretation of the "political purpose" test
of Section 82025. The result of such an interpretation would
be to allow the routine flow of mail by public officials and
others regarding matters of public concern but subject campaign-
oriented mail to the strictures of the Act.

The second issue is the traceability of mail; presumably
enhanced by the possible reading of the section to require the
use of meter or bulk rate mail to the exclusion of postage
stamps.

The problems with such a reading are:

(1) There is no prohibition against the sharing of a postal
meter; thus, if more than one campaign shares a meter there
would be no particular way to identify the sender: in such
an event there is no traceability.

(2) The postal meter number on a mailing is in itself no
guaranty that a recipient of metered mail can identify the
sender. Possibly inquiries could be made of Pitney-Bowes,
however, there is no reason to expect Pitney-Bowes to tell
any person who might call to whom they have rented any particu-
lar machine. If the machine had been purchased it may well
have changed hands several times since the original sale from
the maker.

The Act recognizes this problem and in trying to overcome
it, requires the meter number to be shown on campaign
statements, however:

(3) The campaign reports are not filed by meter
number and one would have to look through thousands
of statements to find the right one. In a local
campaign one would have to know the county in which
the committee files before being able even to look
through the campaign statements.

(4) There are many small local campaigns for city and
county office of various types which are run on short
budgets and should not purposelessly be put to the expense
of either buying a postal bulk rate permit which they might
never again use or the expense of renting a meter (see the
Nevins memo in that regard).

(5) There is no way in which first class bulk rate mail
can be used in any mailing of less than 300 pieces. The
person wishing to send more than 200 but less than 300
pieces of mail must thus either get a meter or use fourth
class mail.

(6) This reading of the Act would implicitly forbid the
use of postcards.

(7) The enforcement of the section would be very difficult
at best. It is rational to claim that any mailing sent by
any person or group regarding any public issue has a "political
purpose" and thus comes under the coverage of the Act if 200
pieces are involved. There must be thousands of organizations
that would be subject to this section.

In order to enforce this section, the Commission would
also have to clarify the definition of a "mass mailing"
found in Section 32041.5 which speaks of "200 pieces of
identical or nearly identical pieces of mail" but does not
address itself to the timing of a mailing. That is, within
what period of time does a mailing of 200 pieces constitute
a mass mailing? Can a sender freely mail 199 pieces today
and 199 pieces a day from today? a week from today? a month
from today? or ever?

There is finally the question of whether there is any public
purpose in interpreting the Act so narrowly as to force the
use of one particular mailing method rather than another for
non-campaign communication, or for campaign mailings where
there is no attempt at anonymity. Such strictures might
seem to be capricious.

Since the traceability of mail is, as indicated above, already
somewhat difficult, and to provide real safeguards against
anonymity, we might be better served by an interpretation
which would allow the use of stamps for mail subject to
Section 24305 so long as the sender shows both on the inside
and outside of the mailing the name and address of the
sender, and, if at the time of the sending of the mail a copy
is required to be sent to the Commission.

Since a better record than is now required would then be
created, it would appear to make little difference which
particular mailing method is utilized and that decision
could continue to depend on the vagaries of campaign management.

If the Commission were to agree that a clarification of
the intended coverages of this section together with the
providing of an alternative method of protecting against
anonymity which does not dictate a mailing method is desirable,
there are two alternative ways of proceeding:

(a) The Commission could seek a legislative
solution by amending a presently introduced
bill which contains an emergency clause. Such
a bill is available.

(b) The Commission could seek a solution by
using its regulatory power; by very narrowly
defining some of the terms used in 84305 and by
deeming an alternative method of mailing to
satisfy the intent of the Act.

Alternative suggestions follow:

(a) Legislation

84305: No person shall make an expenditure for
the purpose of sending a-mass-mailing any mail
the cost of which is reportable pursuant to
this Chapter unless the postage is paid by
postage meter or the mail is sent by first
class-or-third-class bulk rate mail or unless
the sender shows on the outside of each piece
of mail in the mass mailing and on at least
one of the inserts included within each piece
of mail of such mailing the sender's name, street
address and city in no less than 6 point type.
The bulk rate number or meter number shall be
stated in a campaign statement, and a copy of
every mass mailing in support of or in opposition
to a state candidate or state measure shall be
sent to the Commission on the same day on which
it is mailed to the public generally. Such
copies sent to the Commission shall be public
records.

(b) Regulation

(a) For the purposes of Government Code
Section 84305, the term "expenditure" includes
only those payments which are reportable under
any of the provisions of Chapter 4, Article 2
of this Act.

(b) Whenever a person is required to
send a copy of any mass mailing to .the Commission pursuant to Government Code Section 84305,
such person shall:

(1) include a copy of any envelope being
used, showing the postage meter or bulk
rate number;

(2) send such copy to the Commission on
the same day as the mass mailing is being
sent to the public generally.

(c) A person may meet the requirements of
identification, i.e., the use of bulk rate or
postage meters, established by Section 84305 for
any mass mailing proposed to be sent if such
person shows on the outside of the mailing and on
at least one of the inserts included within each
mailing, the name, street address and city of the
sender and provided that the person sent to the
Commission on the same day on which the mailing
is sent to the public generally, one copy of any
such mailing.

5

sections.question_synthetic: What mailings fall within Section 84305's postage meter or bulk rate mail requirements, and how should the Commission interpret the scope of this regulation to balance campaign finance transparency with practical implementation concerns?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC recommends a narrower interpretation of Section 84305 that applies only to mailings supporting or opposing state candidates or measures sent by those required to file campaign statements, and proposes either legislative amendments or regulatory clarifications to allow identification by sender name and address on the mail itself as an alternative to meter or bulk rate requirements.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: No formal Q/C sections present; this is an internal memorandum with analysis and proposed solutions rather than a direct response letter. Document provides extensive legislative and regulatory alternatives at the end. Analysis focuses on interpretation issues and practical enforcement challenges.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What mailings fall within Section 84305's postage meter or bulk rate mail requirements, and how should the Commission interpret the scope of this regulation to balance campaign finance transparency with practical implementation concerns?

CONCLUSION: The FPPC recommends a narrower interpretation of Section 84305 that applies only to mailings supporting or opposing state candidates or measures sent by those required to file campaign statements, and proposes either legislative amendments or regulatory clarifications to allow identification by sender name and address on the mail itself as an alternative to meter or bulk rate requirements.

embedding.summary: Internal FPPC memorandum analyzing Section 84305's mass mailing requirements in response to Controller Kenneth Cory's opinion request, discussing whether the regulation applies to all mailings with political purpose or only campaign-specific communications, and proposing legislative or regulatory solutions that balance traceability requirements with practical implementation concerns.
